problem_summary: 2025 MCM Problem B asked teams to build a sustainable-tourism model for Juneau, Alaska — balancing tourism revenue against ecological and social costs of over-tourism, allocating extra revenue, running sensitivity analysis, transferring the model to another over-touristed site, and delivering a memo to the Juneau Tourist Council. This team built a single optimization model that monetizes environmental and social costs into dollars and solves for optimal tourist caps, taxes, and government investment.
models_used: Multi-objective nonlinear programming collapsed to single-objective via a weighted total social utility function U = P_normal + P_extra + E + S (all weights set to 1, justified as a Hicksian/Kaldor-Hicks improvement)
Contingent Valuation Method (CVM) used as the conceptual device to monetize environmental quality (ERI) and social welfare into dollars so dimensions match profit — the paper's central 'highlight'
Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) via Python pyswarm library (50 particles, 100 max iterations, averaged over 10 runs) to solve the constrained nonlinear program
Cosine seasonal demand model N0(t) = max{-A cos(2pi t/365)+B, 0} fit by integrating over peak (t=121..270) and off-season windows to recover A=16822, B=5514
Logistic (saturating) growth model for diminishing returns on government investment in both environment (Gamma1) and society (Gamma2), solved in closed form
Ecosystem Resilience Index ERI = sum 1/(1+beta N(t)) * ERI_max to capture self-recovery degradation with tourist load
Social Cost of Carbon (SCC = $190/ton, 66.13 kg/tourist/day) to price the environmental human-impact term E1
ARIMA(0,0,1) for forecasting Juneau's unemployment-rate growth (model order chosen via ACF/PACF for d, grid search + BIC for p,q)
Linear regression to forecast 2025 Juneau population (33,460) and median income ($47,401)
Fuzzy statistical method to estimate the unquantifiable negative social impact term S2, with weights beta1/beta2 derived from a 2023 resident survey (beta1=2.155)
Monte Carlo sampling (101 equal-interval points over +/-5% of each variable) for local sensitivity, reported as normalized elasticities s(u,c1)=0.2078 etc.
key_techniques: Dimension unification: converting incommensurable environmental and social 'costs' into dollars via CVM so a multi-objective problem becomes a single additive objective — the linchpin that makes the whole optimization coherent

Decision-variable framing tied directly to real policy levers: peak cap c1, off-season floor c2, total investment I, eco/social split ratio gamma1:gamma2, peak tax x1, off-season subsidy x2 — every output is a lever a council can pull

Decomposition of each pillar into sub-terms (E = human impact E1 + ecosystem resilience E2 + gov investment E3; S = employment S1 + negative externality S2 + gov investment S3), each separately quantified
Continuous approximation of discrete daily sums via integration to make the seasonal-demand fit and objective tractable

Sanity-checking outputs against ground truth: optimal cap 13,993 ~ 5 cruise ships vs current 7, matching residents' stated wish (5:7), and forecasted peak 22,326 — used as validation rather than just asserting
Sensitivity-driven policy ranking: elasticities give c1 > gamma1 > I > c2, turning numbers into a prioritized recommendation (peak cap is the key policy)

Transfer/contrast design: re-parameterizing for Jiuzhaigou (waste cost replacing carbon, different alpha/Gamma) yields qualitatively different policy (no peak cap needed) — directly answering 'how does location change which factors matter'
why_it_won: Judges reward a model that genuinely answers every sub-question with one coherent machine, and this paper's CVM-to-dollars trick lets a messy three-way tradeoff become a single solvable optimization whose outputs are literally the policy knobs the council controls. The validation against real data (5 vs 7 cruise ships) and the sensitivity-based ranking that names peak-capping as THE key policy show modeling maturity beyond just getting numbers. The Jiuzhaigou transfer is not a token re-run but produces a contrasting conclusion (don't cap, do tax/invest), which is exactly the location-dependence insight the prompt demanded.
section_highlights: The summary front-loads concrete numbers (cap 13,993; off-season 1,035; invest $92,310.46 at 54.33:45.67) so a skimming judge instantly sees actionable results, and the two flowcharts (work process + factor decomposition) make a complex additive model legible at a glance. The writing consistently closes the loop from formula to policy meaning, and the standalone memorandum is well-pitched to a non-technical council with rounded figures, cruise-ship equivalents, and an honest request for further study.
pitfalls_or_limitations: CVM is invoked in name only — no actual willingness-to-pay survey was run; key magnitudes (ERI=2e5, Gamma_m=1e5, alpha=1e-4, beta=1e-4) are hand-picked to make terms 'approximately equal,' so the monetization is essentially calibrated by fiat, not measured

Setting all three objective weights a1=a2=a3=1 is asserted as a 'Hicksian improvement' but is really an arbitrary normalization that drives the entire optimum; the result is only as meaningful as those invisible scaling choices
PSO output is unstable: the 10 iterations show wide spread (e.g. c2 ranges 136 to 2000, x2 from 0 to -14,766) and the team simply averages them, which the paper itself admits risks local optima

Internal inconsistency in the peak-demand figure — body integrates to imply ~22,336/16,950 while the memo cites 22,326, and the cosine-fit integration bounds (121..220, 211..365) don't cleanly cover the stated 121..270 peak window

Sensitivity analysis is purely local (small +/-5% perturbations around one operating point), so claimed 'robustness/low sensitivity' doesn't establish global stability and can't detect the local-optimum problem it acknowledges elsewhere
Many parameters (per-capita profit p, carbon per tourist, negative-impact fuzzy value) are single-source point estimates with no uncertainty propagation
Only one transfer city (Jiuzhaigou) tests generalizability, and for it the team drops the S1 constant term and skips computing U, weakening the comparison
reusable_patterns: Monetize-to-unify: when a problem mixes incommensurable objectives (money, ecology, welfare), pick a valuation principle (CVM, SCC, shadow prices) to convert everything to one unit, then optimize a single additive utility — far cleaner than juggling Pareto fronts
Make decision variables literally be policy levers so the optimizer's output is directly the answer the stakeholder needs (caps, taxes, budget splits)
Use saturating (logistic) returns for any 'spend money to get benefit' term so investment doesn't scale unboundedly
Validate the optimum against an external real-world anchor (here: cruise-ship count residents already asked for) and report the match as evidence
Convert sensitivity elasticities into an explicit ranked priority list of policies — judges love a defensible 'this is the single most important action' claim

Design the transfer case to deliberately differ (swap the dominant environmental driver, re-tune saturation parameters) so it yields a contrasting policy and demonstrates real location-dependence rather than a copy-paste re-run
Two-flowchart pattern: one for workflow, one for model-structure decomposition, to make an additive multi-term model readable
Average a stochastic solver (PSO) over multiple seeded runs and report the per-run table for transparency about solution variance
